icd_category: D50.0 — Iron deficiency anemia secondary to blood loss (chronic)
one_liner: A 29-year-old woman presents with 3 months of progressive fatigue, exercise intolerance, and pallor, with a history of heavy menstrual periods lasting 7-8 days.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports progressive fatigue over the past 3 months that has worsened to the point where she feels exhausted after climbing one flight of stairs. She previously exercised regularly at the gym but has had to stop due to shortness of breath and lightheadedness with exertion. She has noticed her skin appears paler than usual and her nails have become brittle. She occasionally craves chewing ice (pica). She reports her menstrual periods have been heavy for the past 2 years — lasting 7-8 days with soaking through a pad every 2-3 hours on the heaviest days (days 2-4), with passage of large clots. She changes pads approximately 10-12 times per day during heavy flow. She denies intermenstrual bleeding. She does not use hormonal contraception. She follows a vegetarian diet. She denies melena, hematochezia, hematuria, or hemoptysis. No weight loss, fever, or night sweats.
stage2.pmh: Menorrhagia (heavy menstrual bleeding for 2 years, no prior evaluation). No prior history of anemia.
stage2.family_history: Mother with uterine fibroids. Sister with this condition. No family history of bleeding disorders, thalassemia, or GI malignancy.
stage2.social_history: Works as a graphic designer. Lives with roommate. Single. Vegetarian for 5 years. Denies tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. Exercises (or used to) 4 times per week. No recent travel. Sexually active with one male partner, uses condoms.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Pale-appearing young woman in no acute distress. HEENT: **Pale conjunctivae bilaterally**; **Smooth, atrophic tongue (glossitis)**; **Angular cheilitis at oral commissures**. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular rhythm, grade 2/6 systolic flow murmur (physiologic)**. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: No edema; **Nails: koilonychia (spoon nails) noted on several fingers, brittle**. Skin: **Pale, no petechiae, no bruising**; Neurological: Grossly intact.

lab_results.result: WBC 6.2, Hgb 8.4, Hct 26.2, MCV 68 fL, MCH 24 pg, MCHC 30 g/dL, RDW 18.2%, Plt 380
lab_results.clinical_significance: Microcytic hypochromic anemia with elevated RDW and reactive thrombocytosis — classic pattern for this condition
Inappropriately low reticulocyte response for degree of anemia, indicating inadequate marrow response due to this condition
Low serum iron confirming this condition (normal 60-170)
Critically low ferritin (normal 12-150) — ferritin <15 is diagnostic of this condition; this is the most sensitive and specific single test
Elevated TIBC (normal 250-370) reflecting increased transferrin production in response to this condition
Very low transferrin saturation (normal 20-50%) confirming this condition

lab_results.result: Microcytic, hypochromic red blood cells with anisocytosis and occasional pencil cells (elliptocytes). No target cells.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Morphology consistent with the suspected diagnosis — pencil cells are characteristic

lab_results.result: Na 140, K 4.0, Cl 103, CO2 25, BUN 10, Cr 0.7, Glucose 85, Ca 9.2, AST 18, ALT 15, Alk Phos 55, Albumin 4.0
lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal metabolic function; no renal or liver disease contributing to anemia

imaging_results.result: Uterus mildly enlarged at 10 cm. Two intramural fibroids, the largest measuring 3.2 cm. Endometrial stripe 8 mm, regular. Bilateral ovaries normal.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Uterine fibroids identified as the likely cause of menorrhagia and chronic blood loss leading to this condition

key_tests: CBC — showing microcytic hypochromic anemia (Hgb 8.4, MCV 68)
low_yield_tests: Celiac panel (no GI symptoms suggesting malabsorption)

stage5.final_clue: The cause is chronic blood loss from menorrhagia (heavy periods for 2 years with pad saturation every 2-3 hours). Pelvic ultrasound reveals uterine fibroids as the etiology of heavy bleeding.

debrief.classic_presentation: A reproductive-age woman with heavy menstrual bleeding presenting with gradual-onset fatigue, exercise intolerance, pallor, and signs specific to iron deficiency: koilonychia (spoon nails), glossitis (smooth tongue), angular cheilitis, and pica (craving non-food items, especially ice — pagophagia). Labs show microcytic hypochromic anemia with low ferritin, low serum iron, elevated TIBC, and low transferrin saturation.
debrief.pathophysiology: Iron is essential for hemoglobin synthesis, oxygen transport, and cellular metabolism. The body contains 3-4 g of total iron, with 65% in hemoglobin. Iron is absorbed in the duodenum and proximal jejunum; absorption is regulated by hepcidin. In iron deficiency, hepcidin levels drop, maximizing iron absorption, but when losses exceed absorption (as in menorrhagia), iron stores are progressively depleted. The sequence is: (1) iron depletion (low ferritin, normal Hgb), (2) iron-deficient erythropoiesis (low transferrin saturation, normal or borderline Hgb), (3) iron deficiency anemia (microcytic hypochromic anemia). Pica and koilonychia result from iron-dependent enzyme deficiency in epithelial tissues. Reactive thrombocytosis occurs because thrombopoietin and erythropoietin share structural homology, and elevated erythropoietin cross-reacts with thrombopoietin receptors.
debrief.treatment_overview: Iron replacement: oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) taken 1-2 times daily on an empty stomach with vitamin C to enhance absorption. Avoid taking with calcium, antacids, tea, or coffee which inhibit absorption. Expect reticulocyte response in 7-10 days and hemoglobin improvement of 1-2 g/dL per month. Continue iron supplementation for 3-6 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replete stores (target ferritin >50). IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, iron sucrose) for patients who cannot tolerate oral iron or need rapid repletion. Address the underlying cause: gynecology referral for fibroid management — options include hormonal therapy (combined OCP, levonorgestrel IUD, tranexamic acid during menses), myomectomy, or uterine artery embolization. Transfusion rarely needed unless symptomatic anemia with Hgb <7 or hemodynamic instability.
high_yield_pearls: Ferritin is the MOST sensitive and specific single test for iron deficiency — a ferritin <15 ng/mL is diagnostic. However, ferritin is an acute phase reactant and may be falsely normal in infection/inflammation.
Menorrhagia is the most common cause of iron deficiency anemia in premenopausal women — always quantify menstrual blood loss (>80 mL/cycle = menorrhagia).
The MCV and RDW help distinguish IDA from thalassemia trait: IDA has elevated RDW (>14.5%) while thalassemia trait typically has normal RDW with a very low MCV.
Koilonychia (spoon nails), pica (ice craving), glossitis, and angular cheilitis are physical exam findings specific to iron deficiency, not just anemia in general.

In premenopausal women with clear menstrual blood loss, GI workup is not routinely needed. In postmenopausal women or men with IDA, GI evaluation (colonoscopy, upper endoscopy) is mandatory to rule out GI malignancy.
sources: AGA Clinical Practice Guideline on the Management of Iron Deficiency Anemia (Gastroenterology, 2020) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=AGA+Clinical+Practice+Guideline+on+the+Management+of+Iron+Deficiency+Anemia+%28Gastroenterology%2C+2020%29
Camaschella C. Iron-deficiency anemia. N Engl J Med, 2015 — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Camaschella+C.+Iron-deficiency+anemia.+N+Engl+J+Med%2C+2015

ACOG Practice Bulletin: Management of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding Associated with Leiomyomata (2021) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=ACOG+Practice+Bulletin%3A+Management+of+Abnormal+Uterine+Bleeding+Associated+with+Leiomyomata+%282021%29

Short MW, Domagalski JE. Iron deficiency anemia: evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician, 2013 — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Short+MW%2C+Domagalski+JE.+Iron+deficiency+anemia%3A+evaluation+and+management.+Am+Fam+Physician%2C+2013
Lopez A et al. Iron deficiency anaemia. Lancet, 2016 — https://www.thelancet.com/action/doSearch?text1=Lopez+A+et+al.+Iron+deficiency+anaemia.+Lancet%2C+2016

after_stage2_more_likely: Iron deficiency anemia — heavy menstrual bleeding, vegetarian diet, pallor, koilonychia, glossitis, pica, tachycardia
Thalassemia trait — microcytic anemia in a young woman, though pica and koilonychia are more specific for iron deficiency
after_stage2_less_likely: Hypothyroidism — no cold intolerance, constipation, or thyromegaly
Vitamin B12 deficiency — would cause macrocytic anemia, not microcytic
Anemia of chronic disease — no chronic inflammatory condition identified
after_labs_top3: Iron deficiency anemia from menorrhagia — microcytic anemia, ferritin 6, low iron, elevated TIBC, uterine fibroids on ultrasound
Iron deficiency with thalassemia trait coexistence — cannot fully exclude without hemoglobin electrophoresis, but iron deficiency is clearly present and sufficient to explain findings
Celiac disease with iron malabsorption — possible contributor but no GI symptoms; menorrhagia is the evident cause
